content.text: In JavaScript, every value has an inherent boolean quality — it behaves like
when evaluated in a boolean context such as an
statement, a ternary, or a logical operator. Values that behave like
. This behavior appears constantly in React, especially in conditional rendering.
There are exactly eight falsy values in JavaScript. Every other value — no matter what type — is truthy:
Anything not in the falsy list is truthy. Several values commonly trip people up:

// Surprising truthy values:
'0'       // truthy — non-empty string, even if it's the character '0'
'false'   // truthy — non-empty string
[]        // truthy — empty array
{}        // truthy — empty object
-1        // truthy — any non-zero number
Infinity  // truthy
function(){} // truthy — functions are objects

if ('0') console.log('truthy!');   // prints 'truthy!'
if ([])  console.log('truthy!');   // prints 'truthy!'
if ({})  console.log('truthy!');   // prints 'truthy!'
JavaScript automatically converts values to boolean in these situations:
&& (AND): returns the first falsy value, or the last value

// && returns the first falsy operand, or the last operand if all are truthy
true && 'hello'     // 'hello'  (left is truthy, returns right)
'foo' && 'bar'      // 'bar'    (both truthy, returns last)
false && 'hello'    // false    (left is falsy, short-circuits)
0 && 'hello'        // 0        (left is falsy, returns 0)
null && doSomething() // null   (doSomething never runs)
|| (OR): returns the first truthy value, or the last value
?? (Nullish Coalescing): only falls back for null/undefined
but only falls back when the left side is

// || falls back for ALL falsy values (0, '', false, null, undefined)
0 || 'default'      // 'default' — 0 is falsy!
'' || 'default'     // 'default' — empty string is falsy!

// ?? only falls back for null and undefined
0 ?? 'default'      // 0        — 0 is kept
'' ?? 'default'     // ''       — empty string is kept
null ?? 'default'   // 'default' — null triggers fallback
undefined ?? 'default' // 'default' — undefined triggers fallback
This distinction matters when a valid value can be
or an empty string. For example, a user's score might legitimately be
, and you don't want it replaced by a default.
Truthy/falsy behavior is the engine behind JSX conditional rendering. React renders
Using || for 0 or empty string defaults
returns the first falsy value or the last value;
returns the first truthy value or the last value.
explicitly convert any value to a boolean.
